subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: As-salaamu alaykum, and welcome to your first Year 4 Science unit! In this unit you are going to become a living things detective. Everywhere you look, from the busy market in Mogadishu to the wide golden savanna near Garissa, you are surrounded by living things. There are goats chewing acacia leaves, weaver birds building nests, tilapia darting through the Jubba River, and tiny ants marching across the ground carrying crumbs bigger than their own bodies. In this unit you will learn how scientists make sense of all this life by sorting living things into groups, and how every plant and animal is perfectly suited to the place it calls home.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of the following. Keep this list nearby and tick each one off when you feel confident.
Explain the difference between living, once-living, and non-living things, and name the seven signs of life.
Sort living things into the two big kingdoms you will study: plants and animals.
Explain what a vertebrate and an invertebrate are, and give examples of each.
Name and describe the five groups of vertebrates: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.
Describe the main groups of plants, including flowering plants and non-flowering plants.
Use a simple classification key to identify an animal by answering yes-or-no questions.
Build and explain a food chain, using the words producer, consumer, predator, and prey.
Explain what a habitat is and describe several East African habitats such as savanna, desert, and mangrove.
Explain how living things show adaptations that help them survive in their habitat.

concepts.title: Is It Living, Once-Living, or Non-Living?
concepts.explanation: Before we can sort living things into groups, we must first answer a simpler question: what makes something alive? This sounds easy, but it can be tricky. A rushing river moves and a flickering fire grows, yet neither is alive. Meanwhile, a seed sitting still in the dry ground looks like it is doing nothing, yet it is very much alive. So how do scientists decide? Scientists use a checklist of seven signs of life. If something does all seven, it is living. A helpful way to remember them is that all living things…
concepts.example: Movement - Living things can move by themselves. A goat walks, a bird flies, and even a sunflower slowly turns to face the sun.

concepts.title: The Big Groups - Plants and Animals
concepts.explanation: Once we know something is alive, the next question is: what kind of living thing is it? Scientists sort all living things into large groups called kingdoms. There are several kingdoms, including tiny living things too small to see, but in Year 4 we will focus on the two you know best: the plant kingdom and the animal kingdom. The clearest difference between plants and animals is how they get their food. Plants make their own food. Using their green leaves, sunlight, water, and a gas from the air called carbon…
concepts.example: Animals cannot make their own food. They must eat other living things to get energy. A cow eats grass, a lion eats a gazelle, and a person eats bariis (rice), goat meat, and dates. Because animals eat other living…

concepts.title: Animals With Backbones and Without
concepts.explanation: The animal kingdom is huge, so scientists split it in two. Stand up straight and reach behind you to feel the middle of your back. Can you feel the row of little bumps running from your neck down to your waist? That is your backbone, also called your spine. It is made of many small bones and it holds your body up. Animals that have a backbone are called vertebrates. Animals that do not have a backbone are called invertebrates. This one feature divides the whole animal kingdom into two enormous camps. A lion, a…
concepts.example: Here is something surprising. Even though the big animals we notice first, like elephants and giraffes, are vertebrates, the truth is that most animals on Earth are invertebrates. For every vertebrate, there are many,…

concepts.title: The Five Groups of Vertebrates
concepts.explanation: All vertebrates share a backbone, but they are not all the same. Scientists sort vertebrates into five groups: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals. Each group has its own set of clues that help you recognise it. Learn these clues well, because you will use them to become a fast, confident classifier. Fish live in water their whole lives. They breathe using gills, which take in oxygen straight from the water, so they do not need to come up for air the way you do. Their bodies are usually covered in…
concepts.example: Amphibians, such as frogs and toads, live part of their life in water and part on land. Their name even means 'two lives'. They have smooth, moist skin with no scales. Baby frogs, called tadpoles, hatch in water and…

concepts.title: The Groups of Plants
concepts.explanation: Just as animals are sorted into groups, so are plants. The simplest way to sort plants is by asking one question: does it make flowers? Flowering plants grow flowers, and their flowers turn into fruits that hold seeds. Most of the plants you eat and see every day are flowering plants: the mango, banana, and papaya trees in a garden, the grasses of the savanna, the acacia with its tiny puffball flowers, and the mighty baobab tree. The flower's job is to help the plant make seeds so that new plants can grow.
concepts.example: Non-flowering plants never make flowers. Instead they make new plants in other ways, often by making tiny dust-like spores. Ferns, which love damp shady corners, and mosses, which form soft green cushions on wet rocks…

concepts.title: Sorting With a Classification Key
concepts.explanation: Scientists have a clever tool for identifying living things called a classification key, or sometimes a 'branching key'. It works by asking a series of yes-or-no questions. Each answer sends you down a different path, until you arrive at the name of your animal. It is a bit like a game of twenty questions. Imagine you find an animal and want to know which vertebrate group it belongs to. You could follow a key like this:
concepts.example: Does it have hair or fur and feed its babies milk? If yes, it is a mammal. If no, go to question 2.

explorations.title: The Living Things Hunt
explorations.context: To observe and sort the living, once-living, and non-living things around your home.
explorations.prompt: Before you begin, write down: Do you think you will find more living, once-living, or non-living things around your home? Make a prediction and give a reason.
explorations.answer: Which column had the most things in it? Was your hypothesis correct? Choose one living thing you found. Which of the seven signs of life could you actually see it doing? Why is a wooden door once-living and not non-living?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe and sort the living, once-living, and non-living things around your home.

explorations.title: Sorting the Animals (Classification Key)
explorations.context: To build and use your own classification key to sort animals into the five vertebrate groups.
explorations.prompt: Predict: Which vertebrate group do you think you will be able to name the most animals for? Why?
explorations.answer: Which group was easiest to fill? Which was hardest? Why do you think so? Was any animal tricky to place? Which clue finally helped you decide? Did your family member's answers match yours when they used your key? If not, how could you make your key clearer?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build and use your own classification key to sort animals into the five vertebrate groups.

explorations.title: How a Camel's Fat Keeps It Going (Water and Storage)
explorations.context: To model how storing something (like fat in a camel's hump) helps an animal survive when supplies run low.
explorations.prompt: Predict: Which cup of water will last longer before drying up, the open one or the covered one? This models how a camel saves water.
explorations.answer: Which cup lost more water? Why do you think this happened? How is the covered cup like a camel that saves water inside its body? Why is saving water so important for animals living in the dry, semi-arid land of the Horn of Africa?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how storing something (like fat in a camel's hump) helps an animal survive when supplies run low.

explorations.title: Do Plants Reach for the Light?
explorations.context: To observe whether a plant responds to light, showing that plants are alive and sense their environment.
explorations.prompt: Predict: If a plant only gets light from one side, which way will it grow?
explorations.answer: Which direction did the plant grow or lean? Why do you think it did this? What happened after you turned the plant around? Which sign of life does this show a plant can do? What does this prove about plants being alive?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe whether a plant responds to light, showing that plants are alive and sense their environment.

explorations.title: Build a Savanna Food Chain
explorations.context: To build and explain food chains using living things from a habitat near you.
explorations.prompt: Predict: What do you think always comes first at the start of a food chain, and why?
explorations.answer: In your chains, which living thing is always the producer? Why must it come first? Pick one animal and say whether it is a predator, prey, or both. Explain how it can sometimes be both. What would happen to the whole chain if the grass and trees died in a drought?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build and explain food chains using living things from a habitat near you.

methods.title: The Living Things Hunt
methods.example: To observe and sort the living, once-living, and non-living things around your home.
steps: Draw a table on your paper with three columns: Living, Once-living, and Non-living.
Walk slowly around your home and garden with an adult.
Each time you spot something, decide which column it belongs in and write it down.
For each living thing, quickly note whether it is a plant or an animal.
Try to find at least five things for each column.

methods.title: Sorting the Animals (Classification Key)
methods.example: To build and use your own classification key to sort animals into the five vertebrate groups.
steps: On your paper, write the five group names: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals.
Think of at least twelve animals you know, including local ones like camel, goat, tilapia, flamingo, crocodile, lizard, and frog.
For each animal, ask the key questions: Does it have hair and feed milk? Feathers? Gills in water? Dry scales and leathery eggs? Then place it in the right group.
Now write your own three-question key that a friend could follow to sort any animal.
Test your key by asking a family member to use it on an animal you choose.

methods.title: How a Camel's Fat Keeps It Going (Water and Storage)
methods.example: To model how storing something (like fat in a camel's hump) helps an animal survive when supplies run low.
steps: Fill both cups with the same amount of water and mark the level on the outside of each.
Leave one cup open. Cover the second cup loosely with the cloth or saucer.
Place both cups on a warm, sunny windowsill.
Each day for three days, mark the new water level in each cup.
Compare how much water is left in each cup after three days.

methods.title: Do Plants Reach for the Light?
methods.example: To observe whether a plant responds to light, showing that plants are alive and sense their environment.
steps: If using seeds, plant two or three beans in a cup of soil and water them lightly. If using a potted plant, simply use it as it is.
Place the cup or pot on a windowsill so that light comes mainly from one side.
Water the soil a little each day to keep it damp, not soaked.
Each day for one week, look at the direction the stem and leaves are pointing and draw what you see.
After a few days, turn the plant around so the light comes from the other side, and keep watching.

methods.title: Build a Savanna Food Chain
methods.example: To build and explain food chains using living things from a habitat near you.
steps: On separate small cards, write or draw these living things: grass, gazelle, lion, grasshopper, lizard, eagle, acacia tree, and the sun.
Arrange the cards to make a food chain, placing the sun and a plant first.
Draw arrows between the cards. Remember: each arrow points from the food to the eater.
Now make a second, different food chain using the same cards in a new order where it makes sense.
Label each card as a producer, a consumer, a predator, or prey.

workedExamples.title: Classifying an Unknown Animal
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through a full example together, step by step, just like a scientist would. Imagine your cousin Yusuf describes an animal he saw near the river: 'It had dry scaly skin, four short legs, it laid leathery eggs on the sandy bank, and it warmed itself…
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Vertebrate or invertebrate? It has legs and a solid body with a backbone shape, and later we hear it is large and strong. It is a vertebrate. Step 2 - Use the key. Hair and milk? No mention of fur, and reptiles do not feed milk. So it is not a mammal. Step 3 - Feathers? No feathers are described. So it is not a bird. Step 4 - Gills in water for its whole life? No, it comes onto the sandy bank and suns itself. So it is not a fish.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is an invertebrate? (a) camel (b) tilapia (c) ant (d) flamingo
workedExamples.solution: (c) ant. An ant has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are all vertebrates.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which vertebrate group has feathers? (a) mammals (b) reptiles (c) birds (d) fish
workedExamples.solution: (c) birds. Feathers are found only on birds; no other animal group has them.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: A camel belongs to which vertebrate group? (a) reptiles (b) mammals (c) birds (d) amphibians
workedExamples.solution: (b) mammals. A camel has hair and feeds its young milk, which are mammal features.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a producer? (a) lion (b) gazelle (c) acacia tree (d) eagle
workedExamples.solution: (c) acacia tree. A plant makes its own food from sunlight, so it is a producer.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: In the food chain grass -> gazelle -> lion, the lion is the: (a) producer (b) prey (c) predator (d) plant
workedExamples.solution: (c) predator. The lion hunts and eats the gazelle, so it is the predator.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A frog that lives partly in water and partly on land is a: (a) fish (b) amphibian (c) reptile (d) bird
workedExamples.solution: (b) amphibian. Amphibians such as frogs live partly in water and partly on land.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the seven signs of life? (a) growing (b) feeding (c) being made of metal (d) reproducing
workedExamples.solution: (c) being made of metal. Metal is not a sign of life. The seven signs include growing, feeding, and reproducing.

practice.prompt: An animal with a backbone is called a: (a) invertebrate (b) vertebrate (c) producer (d) predator
practice.answer: (b) vertebrate. A vertebrate is an animal with a backbone, or spine.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is an invertebrate? (a) camel (b) tilapia (c) ant (d) flamingo
practice.answer: (c) ant. An ant has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are all vertebrates.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which vertebrate group has feathers? (a) mammals (b) reptiles (c) birds (d) fish
practice.answer: (c) birds. Feathers are found only on birds; no other animal group has them.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A camel belongs to which vertebrate group? (a) reptiles (b) mammals (c) birds (d) amphibians
practice.answer: (b) mammals. A camel has hair and feeds its young milk, which are mammal features.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a producer? (a) lion (b) gazelle (c) acacia tree (d) eagle
practice.answer: (c) acacia tree. A plant makes its own food from sunlight, so it is a producer.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In the food chain grass -> gazelle -> lion, the lion is the: (a) producer (b) prey (c) predator (d) plant
practice.answer: (c) predator. The lion hunts and eats the gazelle, so it is the predator.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A frog that lives partly in water and partly on land is a: (a) fish (b) amphibian (c) reptile (d) bird
practice.answer: (b) amphibian. Amphibians such as frogs live partly in water and partly on land.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the seven signs of life? (a) growing (b) feeding (c) being made of metal (d) reproducing
practice.answer: (c) being made of metal. Metal is not a sign of life. The seven signs include growing, feeding, and reproducing.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A habitat is best described as: (a) a type of food (b) the natural home of a living thing (c) a group of bones (d) a kind of plant
practice.answer: (b) the natural home of a living thing. A habitat provides food, water, shelter, and space.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The camel's hump is an example of a: (a) food chain (b) predator (c) habitat (d) adaptation
practice.answer: (d) adaptation. The hump is a body feature that helps the camel survive in the desert.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which animal is a reptile? (a) frog (b) crocodile (c) flamingo (d) tilapia
practice.answer: (b) crocodile. A crocodile has dry scaly skin and lays leathery eggs on land, so it is a reptile.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Plants make their own food using sunlight in a process called: (a) migration (b) photosynthesis (c) classification (d) reproduction
practice.answer: (b) photosynthesis. This is how green plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Living Things Hunt
activities.materials: A pencil and paper or notebook; A safe area to walk around, such as your home, yard, or garden; An adult to walk with you
steps: Draw a table on your paper with three columns: Living, Once-living, and Non-living.
Walk slowly around your home and garden with an adult.
Each time you spot something, decide which column it belongs in and write it down.
For each living thing, quickly note whether it is a plant or an animal.
Try to find at least five things for each column.

activities.title: Sorting the Animals (Classification Key)
activities.materials: Paper and pencil; Your own knowledge of animals, or a picture book or family photos of animals if you have them
steps: On your paper, write the five group names: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals.
Think of at least twelve animals you know, including local ones like camel, goat, tilapia, flamingo, crocodile, lizard, and frog.
For each animal, ask the key questions: Does it have hair and feed milk? Feathers? Gills in water? Dry scales and leathery eggs? Then place it in the right group.
Now write your own three-question key that a friend could follow to sort any animal.
Test your key by asking a family member to use it on an animal you choose.

activities.title: How a Camel's Fat Keeps It Going (Water and Storage)
activities.materials: Two identical small cups or jars; Water; A small piece of cloth or a saucer to act as a lid; A sunny windowsill; A pencil to mark water levels
steps: Fill both cups with the same amount of water and mark the level on the outside of each.
Leave one cup open. Cover the second cup loosely with the cloth or saucer.
Place both cups on a warm, sunny windowsill.
Each day for three days, mark the new water level in each cup.
Compare how much water is left in each cup after three days.

activities.title: Do Plants Reach for the Light?
activities.materials: A few dried beans or maize seeds, or a small potted plant you already have; A cup or small pot with soil; Water; A window that gets sunlight from one side
steps: If using seeds, plant two or three beans in a cup of soil and water them lightly. If using a potted plant, simply use it as it is.
Place the cup or pot on a windowsill so that light comes mainly from one side.
Water the soil a little each day to keep it damp, not soaked.
Each day for one week, look at the direction the stem and leaves are pointing and draw what you see.
After a few days, turn the plant around so the light comes from the other side, and keep watching.

activities.title: Build a Savanna Food Chain
activities.materials: Paper and pencil, or small pieces of paper (cards); Coloured pencils if you have them
steps: On separate small cards, write or draw these living things: grass, gazelle, lion, grasshopper, lizard, eagle, acacia tree, and the sun.
Arrange the cards to make a food chain, placing the sun and a plant first.
Draw arrows between the cards. Remember: each arrow points from the food to the eater.
Now make a second, different food chain using the same cards in a new order where it makes sense.
Label each card as a producer, a consumer, a predator, or prey.

activities.title: Observe: Sorting With a Classification Key
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of sorting with a classification key around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Scales, gills, fins; cold-blooded; live in water
Moist skin; cold-blooded; water and land; soft eggs in water
Dry scales; cold-blooded; leathery eggs on land
Feathers, beak, wings; warm-blooded; hard-shelled eggs
Hair or fur; warm-blooded; feed milk; usually live young
Warm-blooded vertebrate with hair or fur that feeds its babies milk.
Warm-blooded vertebrate with feathers, wings, and a beak.
Cold-blooded vertebrate with dry, scaly skin; lays leathery eggs on land.
Cold-blooded vertebrate with moist skin; lives in water and on land.
Cold-blooded vertebrate that lives in water and breathes with gills.

vocabulary.example: Each group has its own set of clues that help you recognise it.

vocabulary.meaning: Scales, gills, fins; cold-blooded; live in water
vocabulary.example: Have you ever wondered why a camel can walk for days across the hot, dry semi-arid land without drinking, while a fish would die within minutes out of water?

vocabulary.meaning: Moist skin; cold-blooded; water and land; soft eggs in water
vocabulary.example: Amphibians, such as frogs and toads, live part of their life in water and part on land.

vocabulary.meaning: Dry scales; cold-blooded; leathery eggs on land
vocabulary.example: Putting a camel with mammals and a snake with reptiles.

vocabulary.meaning: Feathers, beak, wings; warm-blooded; hard-shelled eggs
vocabulary.example: There are goats chewing acacia leaves, weaver birds building nests, tilapia darting through the Jubba River, and tiny ants marching across the ground carrying crumbs bigger than their own bodies.

vocabulary.meaning: Hair or fur; warm-blooded; feed milk; usually live young
vocabulary.example: Putting a camel with mammals and a snake with reptiles.
Explain what a vertebrate and an invertebrate are, and give examples of each.

vocabulary.meaning: Warm-blooded vertebrate with hair or fur that feeds its babies milk.
vocabulary.example: It is a mammal that breathes air and feeds its baby milk, just like a camel.

vocabulary.meaning: Warm-blooded vertebrate with feathers, wings, and a beak.
vocabulary.example: A goat walks, a bird flies, and even a sunflower slowly turns to face the sun.

vocabulary.meaning: Cold-blooded vertebrate with dry, scaly skin; lays leathery eggs on land.
vocabulary.example: The Nile crocodile resting on a riverbank is a famous African reptile.

vocabulary.meaning: Cold-blooded vertebrate with moist skin; lives in water and on land.
Cold-blooded vertebrate that lives in water and breathes with gills.
vocabulary.example: Have you ever wondered why a camel can walk for days across the hot, dry semi-arid land without drinking, while a fish would die within minutes out of water?

commonMistakes: It lives in the sea like a fish.
The big animals we notice have bones.
Food chain arrow points to the food.
It seems to point at what is eaten.

connections.text: You will explore ecosystems and how living things depend on one another, building directly on food chains and habitats.
Habitats like savanna, desert, and coast link to the landscapes and climates of East Africa.
Understanding animals as living things helps you understand your own body and how to keep it healthy.
Caring for animals, plants, and habitats is part of being a good khalifah, a caretaker of the earth.
Keep a nature notebook. Each week, draw and classify one new living thing you see.
Visit or read about a nearby habitat, such as a river, a coast, or the savanna, and list the living things there.

fluency.prompt: An animal with a backbone is called a: (a) invertebrate (b) vertebrate (c) producer (d) predator
fluency.answer: (b) vertebrate. A vertebrate is an animal with a backbone, or spine.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) vertebrate. A vertebrate is an animal with a backbone, or spine.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is an invertebrate? (a) camel (b) tilapia (c) ant (d) flamingo
fluency.answer: (c) ant. An ant has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are all vertebrates.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) ant. An ant has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are all vertebrates.

fluency.prompt: Which vertebrate group has feathers? (a) mammals (b) reptiles (c) birds (d) fish
fluency.answer: (c) birds. Feathers are found only on birds; no other animal group has them.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) birds. Feathers are found only on birds; no other animal group has them.

fluency.prompt: A camel belongs to which vertebrate group? (a) reptiles (b) mammals (c) birds (d) amphibians
fluency.answer: (b) mammals. A camel has hair and feeds its young milk, which are mammal features.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) mammals. A camel has hair and feeds its young milk, which are mammal features.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is a producer? (a) lion (b) gazelle (c) acacia tree (d) eagle
fluency.answer: (c) acacia tree. A plant makes its own food from sunlight, so it is a producer.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) acacia tree. A plant makes its own food from sunlight, so it is a producer.

fluency.prompt: In the food chain grass -> gazelle -> lion, the lion is the: (a) producer (b) prey (c) predator (d) plant
fluency.answer: (c) predator. The lion hunts and eats the gazelle, so it is the predator.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) predator. The lion hunts and eats the gazelle, so it is the predator.

fluency.prompt: A frog that lives partly in water and partly on land is a: (a) fish (b) amphibian (c) reptile (d) bird
fluency.answer: (b) amphibian. Amphibians such as frogs live partly in water and partly on land.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) amphibian. Amphibians such as frogs live partly in water and partly on land.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the seven signs of life? (a) growing (b) feeding (c) being made of metal (d) reproducing
fluency.answer: (c) being made of metal. Metal is not a sign of life. The seven signs include growing, feeding, and reproducing.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) being made of metal. Metal is not a sign of life. The seven signs include growing, feeding, and reproducing.

fluency.prompt: A habitat is best described as: (a) a type of food (b) the natural home of a living thing (c) a group of bones (d) a kind of plant
fluency.answer: (b) the natural home of a living thing. A habitat provides food, water, shelter, and space.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) the natural home of a living thing. A habitat provides food, water, shelter, and space.

fluency.prompt: The camel's hump is an example of a: (a) food chain (b) predator (c) habitat (d) adaptation
fluency.answer: (d) adaptation. The hump is a body feature that helps the camel survive in the desert.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (d) adaptation. The hump is a body feature that helps the camel survive in the desert.

fluency.prompt: Which animal is a reptile? (a) frog (b) crocodile (c) flamingo (d) tilapia
fluency.answer: (b) crocodile. A crocodile has dry scaly skin and lays leathery eggs on land, so it is a reptile.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) crocodile. A crocodile has dry scaly skin and lays leathery eggs on land, so it is a reptile.

fluency.prompt: Plants make their own food using sunlight in a process called: (a) migration (b) photosynthesis (c) classification (d) reproduction
fluency.answer: (b) photosynthesis. This is how green plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) photosynthesis. This is how green plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: A goat is a mammal (it has hair and feeds its babies milk) and a chicken is a bird (it has feathers, a beak, and lays hard-shelled eggs). Amina can tell them apart by looking for hair versus feathers.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A goat is a mammal (it has hair and feeds its babies milk) and a chicken is a bird (it has feathers, a beak, and lays hard-shelled eggs). Amina can tell them apart by looking for hair versus feathers.

realProblems.prompt: Amina keeps goats and chickens at her home near Hargeisa. Which vertebrate group does each animal belong to, and how can she tell them apart?
realProblems.answer: When the grass dies, the gazelles lose their food and may starve or move away, so their numbers fall. With fewer gazelles, the lions then have less prey and they too go hungry. This shows how every part of a food chain depends on the plants at the start.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: When the grass dies, the gazelles lose their food and may starve or move away, so their numbers fall. With fewer gazelles, the lions then have less prey and they too go hungry. This shows how every part of a food chain depends on the plants at the start.

realProblems.prompt: During a long drought, the grass on the savanna dries up and dies. Explain what might happen to the gazelles and then to the lions. Use the idea of a food chain.
realProblems.answer: It is most likely a reptile. The clues are its dry, scaly skin and the fact that it is basking in the sun to warm its cold-blooded body.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: It is most likely a reptile. The clues are its dry, scaly skin and the fact that it is basking in the sun to warm its cold-blooded body.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan finds a small animal with dry, scaly skin sunning itself on a warm rock. Which vertebrate group is it most likely from, and what clue tells you?
realProblems.answer: Any two of: the tilapia has gills to take oxygen from water, fins and a tail to swim, scales to protect its body, and a smooth streamlined shape to move easily through water.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Any two of: the tilapia has gills to take oxygen from water, fins and a tail to swim, scales to protect its body, and a smooth streamlined shape to move easily through water.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman on the Jubba River catches tilapia. Explain two ways the tilapia is adapted for living in water.

Scientists would need to study it closely and may need a new or special group. This shows that living things do not always…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the main difference between a vertebrate and an invertebrate?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A vertebrate has a backbone (spine) inside its body; an invertebrate has no backbone. This is the key difference.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the five groups of vertebrates.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The five groups are fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain how plants and animals get their food differently.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Plants make their own food from sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide through photosynthesis, so they are producers. Animals cannot make food and must eat other living things, so they are consumers.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is an adaptation? Give one example from an East African animal.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An adaptation is a body feature or behaviour that helps a living thing survive in its habitat. Example: a camel's hump stores fat for energy and water in the dry desert (other correct examples include the giraffe's long neck or the elephant's trunk).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why must every food chain begin with a plant?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Because plants are the only producers; they capture the sun's energy and make food. Without a plant at the start, there would be no energy entering the chain for animals to use.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name three different habitats found in East Africa.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any three of: savanna, desert or semi-arid land, mangrove swamp, river, coral reef (other reasonable East African habitats are also acceptable).

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of the following. Keep this list nearby and tick each one off when you feel confident.
I can explain the difference between living, once-living, and non-living things, and name the seven signs of life.
I can sort living things into the two big kingdoms you will study: plants and animals.
I can explain what a vertebrate and an invertebrate are, and give examples of each.
I can name and describe the five groups of vertebrates: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.
I can describe the main groups of plants, including flowering plants and non-flowering plants.
I can use a simple classification key to identify an animal by answering yes-or-no questions.
I can build and explain a food chain, using the words producer, consumer, predator, and prey.
